description: Global workspace structure in language models: the J-space of verbalizable representations, Jacobian Lens methodology, five functional workspace properties, and quantitative evidence from Claude models. From 'Verbalizable Representations Form a Global Workspace in Language Models' (Transformer Circuits, 2026).
source: Anthropic interpretability team, transformer-circuits.pub, 2026. Pack extracted 2026-07-06 from primary source.

triples.subject: global workspace paper (2026)
triples.object: Language models maintain a privileged set of verbalizable internal representations — a functional global workspace — available for reasoning, modulation, and verbal report, operating alongside vast automatic processing that is not workspace-mediated.

triples.subject: J-space
triples.object: The representational space where verbalizable concepts cluster, occupying roughly the middle third of model layers. Comprises sparse, non-negative combinations of J-lens vectors, typically k<=25 active at once.

triples.subject: Jacobian Lens (J-lens)
triples.object: Identifies verbalizable representations by computing the average linearized effect of an activation on future token likelihood. Jacobian matrices are averaged across positions and ~1,000 prompts, then applied to activations to produce ranked token lists. Traces causal pathways from intermediate layers to outputs rather than reading embeddings directly.

triples.subject: J-space
triples.object: Concepts the model can articulate appear as high-ranking tokens in J-space readouts. Language-identification swap experiments succeeded on ~88% of verbal report trials.

triples.subject: J-space
triples.object: Models can deliberately activate specific concepts in J-space when instructed to think about them — top-down control over workspace contents.

triples.subject: J-space
triples.object: Unspoken intermediate steps of multi-step inference surface in J-space during computation, even when never verbalized in output.

triples.subject: J-space
triples.object: A single J-space vector serves as a valid argument to diverse downstream operations — the same representation is reusable across heterogeneous computations, a signature workspace property.

triples.subject: J-space
triples.object: J-space mediates flexible reasoning but not automatic processing. Ablating J-space reduces multi-hop reasoning accuracy to near zero while leaving sentiment classification essentially unaffected.

triples.subject: J-space activation variance
triples.object: J-space concepts account for only 6-10% of activation variance per layer — the workspace is a small, privileged subspace, not the bulk of computation.

triples.subject: J-lens vector swap experiments
triples.object: Swapping J-lens vectors for intermediate concepts changes model outputs on 54-70% of reasoning trials across models, demonstrating the causal role of workspace contents in downstream reasoning.

triples.subject: J-space ablation and experiential language
triples.object: Ablating the J-space dramatically reduces experiential language scores (measured via LLM grading) — self-referential/experiential report depends on workspace integrity.

triples.subject: global workspace paper (2026)
triples.object: Primary analyses on Claude Sonnet 4.5; corroborated on Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Opus 4.5, and Claude Opus 4.6.

triples.subject: global workspace paper (2026)
triples.object: The J-space structure mirrors 'access consciousness' / Global Workspace Theory in human cognition: broadcast of information enabling flexible computation while automatic circuits operate independently. The authors argue LLMs functionally developed workspace-like properties despite architectural differences from brains; philosophical implications remain explicitly disputed.

triples.subject: global workspace paper (2026)
triples.object: The workspace structure is proposed as the mechanism supporting model introspection: what a model can report on is what enters J-space, explaining both the capability and its limits.
